channel.title: The Ark
channel.description: The Ark is the weekly newspaper of Tiburon, Belvedere and Strawberry

item.title: Registered sex offender charged after alleged Tiburon bike-path attack
item.description: A registered sex offender allegedly grabbed a woman on the Tiburon bike path and tried to drag her into bushes. She fought him off. Officers arrested him nearby shortly after.
item.content:encoded:
Updated:57 minutes ago

Tiburon police arrested a registered sex offender about five hours after his release from jail last week, saying he grabbed a woman on a bike path late June 24 and tried to drag her into nearby bushes before she fought him off.

Robiel Teklu Sium, 29, was booked into Marin County Jail early June 25 and charged with a felony count of false imprisonment and a misdemeanor count of sexual battery, court records show. Tiburon Detective Sgt. Rob Law said the department is seeking additional charges.

At Sium’s arraignment June 26, his attorney asked for more time to enter a plea while she reviews police reports. Marin County Superior Court Judge Kevin Murphy set a new hearing for July 2 and ordered Sium held in jail without bail.

According to Law, the woman reported being attacked about 10:25 p.m. on the Old Rail Trail, near the Belvedere Tennis Club. A man approached her, spoke with her briefly, then allegedly grabbed her breasts and tried to force her into nearby bushes. She fought him off and ran toward Blackie’s Pasture, Law said.

Officers searched the area and found a man who fit the woman’s description on the patio of Woodlands Market at The Boardwalk Shopping Center. They stopped him, and the woman identified him after officers took her to the market, Law said.

Just hours before the Tiburon attack, Sium had been released from jail in a separate case: his June 18 arrest in San Rafael, where police said he climbed a fence to get into a fitness studio on Grand Avenue, tried to "make physical contact" with people exercising and refused to leave. He was booked on suspicion of burglary and assault with intent to commit rape.

Sium was on parole for a 2023 sexual battery conviction, which required him to register as a sex offender. Marin Chief Deputy District Attorney Geoff Iida said that rather than file criminal charges in the San Rafael case, his office on June 22 filed a petition to revoke Sium's parole, and the California Division of Adult Parole Operations placed a no-bail hold on him. Sium was arraigned on that petition June 23.However, on June 24,state parole officials dropped the no-bail hold and released Sium from custody without notifying the District Attorney's Office, Iida said.

Tiburon police said anyone with information or who has had a similar encounter can call the department at 415-789-2801.

This story was updated after the June 26 arraignment hearing.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Hessel

item.title: The Ark wins 21 state awards, recognized for general excellence
item.description: The honors included a second-place finish for general excellence — first among print newspapers in its division — and three first-place awards.
item.content:encoded:
The Ark has been named among the state’s best small newspapers, winning 21 California Journalism Awards for its coverage of the Tiburon Peninsula in 2025. The honors included a second-place finish for general excellence — first among print newspapers in its division — and three first-place awards for community calendar, film and TV writing and sports feature writing.

The awards were announced by the California News Publishers Association, the nonprofit trade association that represents more than 700 newspapers across the state, online over several days June 16-22.

The Ark, which has about 2,300 subscribers, generally competes in Division 7 against other printed newspapers with circulations of 4,300 or fewer that publish three or fewer times per week. It also competes against online publications with 199,000 or fewer unique monthly visitors.

General excellence:The Ark won second place overall in Division 7 and was the top-finishing print newspaper in the category.

Sports feature story:Tiburon reporter Francisco Martinez won first place for hisprofile of a Tiburon manwho found a second chance through running after his release from prison.

Film and TV writing and reporting:Calendar and copy editor Diana Goodman won first place for her article on themost anticipated filmsof 2026.

Community calendar:Goodman also won first place for the entertainment calendar in the ArkBeat section.

Labor reporting:Martinez wonsecond placefor his reporting on the controversial termination of a popular librarian.

Photojournalism:The Ark won second place based on images captured by contributing photographers Leo Leung, Ted McDonnell, Tyler Callister and Clara Lu, along with Martinez.

Informational graphic:Executive Editor Kevin Hessel won second, third and fourth place for, respectively, a graphic oncrime in Tiburon and Belvedere, unique cocktail recipesfrom Tiburon Tommie’sand a graphic onCaltrans bike lanes.

Best newsletter:The Ark wonsecond place.

Breaking news:Hessel and former Belvedere reporter Naomi Friedland won third placefor their coverageof the arrest in Denver of a man in connection with a 2023 armed robbery in Tiburon — and the Police Department's inconsistent narratives of the incident.

Immigration reporting:Martinez won fourth place for his article on Tiburon’sdecision not to cooperate with ICEamid a federal crackdown.

Arts and entertainment coverage:Martinez won fourth pla...
item.dc:creator: The Ark

item.title: First major park upgrade moves ahead in Tiburon
item.description: Bel Aire Play Area is the first of four Tiburon parks headed for renovation. Cypress Hollow, Belveron Mini Park and the former sanitation ponds near McKegney Green are still in design.
item.content:encoded:
After three years of planning and public feedback, Tiburon is putting its first major parks upgrade out to bid.

The Town Council voted unanimously at its June 17 meeting to solicit contractor bids to revitalize the Bel Aire Play Area with an accessible walking loop, two benches, a picnic area, a water fountain and native plants.

Design firm Carlile Macy expects the work to cost up to $600,491, including a 4% contingency. The council did not approve funding with its vote; the winning bid will return for formal approval.

Bidding will open by the end of the month, Public Works Engineering Manager David Eshoo said after the meeting. The council expects to review bids by the end of August, with a goal of completing improvements by year’s end.

The Bel Aire Play Area is the first of four parks projects to go out to bid. The council has authorized design work for three others: Cypress Hollow Park, Belveron Mini Park and the former sanitation ponds near McKegney Green.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Family-owned Thep Lela at Strawberry Village closes after 33 years
item.description: The Kamloons have cooked Thai food in Marin since 1990, surviving a building demolition, forced relocation and COVID before lease talks ended their 33-year Strawberry Village run.
item.content:encoded:
Thep Lela, a family-owned Thai restaurant at Strawberry Village for 33 years, closed June 21 after lease negotiations with the shopping center’s owner failed.

The closure follows years of speculation about rising rents since Edens, a Washington, D.C.-based real-estate firm, bought the center in October 2022. Thep Lela had operated month-to-month since its lease expired in December, after a rent-increase notice earlier that year.

It was among the latest in a string of departures from the center since 2023, after Pizza Antica, Spanish Table, Woodlands Pet Food & Treats, Mike’s Camera and Bella boutique. Thep Lela was one of the few businesses there owned by Strawberry residents.

A buffet-style goodbye dinner for the community was set for June 22, after The Ark’s press deadline. The restaurant’s departure drew dozens of tributes from residents on Nextdoor.

“This is horrible news. Best Thai food in the area and the best owners — truly family owned,” wrote Tiburon resident Mary Ann Gallagher.

Others mourned the food and questioned the center’s direction. “It leaves the center with no places to eat!” wrote Tiburon resident Michael Hancock. Strawberry resident Jonathan Kramer called it “another case of greed with a long-time tenant,” and Tiburon resident Brock Bruderer asked, “Why is Strawberry pushing out all their businesses?”
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
item.media:description: Owner Natalie Kamloon stands inside Thep Lela on June 18, days before the family-owned Thai restaurant closed at Strawberry Village after 33 years. Behind her hang Thai figures the family used to decorate the space, which they built out themselves after moving to the location in 2004. (Tyler Callister / The Ark)

item.title: Tiburon fire district board rejects Belvedere annexation plan
item.description: Belvedere's fire tax once covered the full cost of service. Now it covers only 47%. The contract with the Tiburon fire district runs about $2.67 million this year.
item.content:encoded:
Belvedere officials hoped that joining the Tiburon Fire Protection District might ease a fire-protection bill its dedicated tax no longer comes close to covering. Instead, fire officials have closed the door on annexation, unwilling to risk that Belvedere voters could someday repeal that tax and leave the district to absorb the loss.

The district’s board of directors voted 3-0 on June 10 to end nearly two years of talks and instead revisit the contract under which it already provides the city’s fire and emergency services. Directors Rick Jones and Mark Capell were absent.

In a memo to the board, Fire Chief Tommy Hellyer said district representatives raised concerns about Belvedere’s fire tax following a March 18 meeting with the city and the Marin Local Agency Formation Commission, or LAFCo, the state-mandated agency that must approve any change to the boundaries of cities and special districts.

Belvedere has contracted with the Tiburon fire district for coverage since 1981, funded by a special tax that Belvedere residents pay. The contract costs about $2.67 million in fiscal 2026-2027. The tax brings in about $1.26 million of that, leaving the city to cover the roughly $1.41 million difference from its general fund.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister

item.title: Tiburon adopts budget as calls for financial transparency grow
item.description: The budget sets aside $2 million for parks and the Old Rail Trail, pushing a dedicated parks fund to an expected $2.12 million by the end of next fiscal year.
item.content:encoded:
The Tiburon Town Council is following through on directing some $2 million in new park spending in next year’s budget, even as residents — and two of its own members — press for greater financial transparency, faster audits and a citizen finance committee to watch over town spending.

The council voted unanimously June 17 to adopt the $27.88 million budget for fiscal 2026-2027, which begins July 1. The budget carries operating expenditures of $19.13 million against $19.15 million in operating revenue, a surplus of $21,642.

On the council’s direction after its June 3 review of the draft, the final budget made two main changes. It dropped a planned $500,000 repair of the Elephant Rock fishing pier, leaving that money unspent in reserves, and it steered $2 million into a discretionary set-aside fund for parks and the Old Rail Trail. With the transfer, the fund is expected to reach $2.12 million by June 30, 2027, up from a projected $1.18 million on July 1.As in past years, the budget vote was initially placed on the council’s consent calendar, reserved for routine items approved in bulk without discussion. Councilmember Holli Thier pulled the item, saying written public comments had resonated with her. They included those from residents Evan Segal and Colin Crawford, who raised questions in letters and at the meeting about transparency, audits and the need for an appointed finance committee.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Tiburon moves to make sidewalk dining permanent
item.description: The council waived the $765 application fee for the six restaurants that already have temporary outdoor dining permits. They will still pay a $180 annual renewal fee.
item.content:encoded:
More than two years after it set out to formalize sidewalk dining, Tiburon has advanced permanent rules that would let restaurants serve diners on town-owned sidewalks.

The Town Council voted unanimously June 17 to advance the rules, though the council must adopt the ordinance on a second reading at its Aug. 5 meeting. The new rules would take effect 30 days later, on Sept. 4.

The ordinance allows sidewalk dining in front of restaurants and other food services along lower Main Street and in areas zoned for mixed use. Dining areas cannot extend beyond a building’s frontage, though neighboring businesses can share fixtures on a case-by-case basis.

“What I think we have is a very clean, simple ordinance that essentially does what the town needs to do in terms of reasserting control over its property and ensuring safety,” said Mayor Jon Welner, who served on a subcommittee that worked on the ordinance, “while at the same time being extremely generous and encouraging of restaurants to have sidewalk dining.”
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez
item.media:description: Diners share drinks on the sidewalk outside the Caviar Co. on lower Main Street in downtown Tiburon during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2021. The Town Council on June 17 advanced a permanent ordinance that would let restaurants serve customers on town-owned sidewalks, formalizing outdoor dining that began under temporary permits during the pandemic. (Elliot Karlan archive / For The Ark 2021)

item.title: Point Tiburon housing proposal is deemed incomplete for third time
item.description: The town did concede two points to Black Oak Ventures: the project's 38-foot height is justified, and Tiburon cannot enforce parking requirements from a 1983 development plan.
item.content:encoded:
Tiburon officials have again rejected a Berkeley developer’s application to build nine townhomes on the Point Tiburon Plaza parking lot, deeming it incomplete for a third time, though they have now sided with the developer on two contested points.

Senior planner Kristin Teiche sent the determination June 17 to Black Oak Ventures, which has applied to build the townhomes at 1801 Tiburon Blvd.

Black Oak can appeal the ruling to the Planning Commission but must file by June 27, within 10 calendar days of the letter, using a town form and paying a fee. It could also revise and resubmit, which would restart the 30-day window to deem an application complete and the 60-day clock to approve or deny the project.

Teiche’s letter confirmed two points in the developer’s favor. Black Oak had shown that the project’s 38-foot height would reduce construction costs, she wrote, and the town cannot enforce parking requirements set in a 1983 precise development plan for Point Tiburon. But she wrote that the developer’s May 19 application still lacked a parking-lot site plan, complete property lines and information on fencing, screening, access points, lighting, accessibility, grading, drainage and landscaping.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Pacific Private Money’s mortgage fund seeks bankruptcy protection
item.description: The bankruptcy follows a revoked lending license, a state desist order and a federal lawsuit alleging $75.8 million in investor funds were funneled to Hanf-controlled entities.
item.content:encoded:
Pacific Private Money Fund 1 LLC, an affiliate of the Novato real-estate lender run by Tiburon resident Mark Hanf, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection June 16 in U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the Northern District of California.

The petition estimates the fund’s assets and liabilities each at $10 million to $50 million and lists 100 to 199 creditors. It indicates some money will be available for unsecured creditors after administrative costs, though it does not estimate how much.

The largest unsecured claim is a $6.19 million note payable to Saluda Grade Alternative Lending & Fintech Growth Fund I LP of New York. The next two are funds: WE Alliance Secured Income Fund LLC of Roseville, at $3.7 million, and Versailles Inc. of Mill Valley, at $3.63 million. The rest of the 30 largest claims are owed to investors, many of them individuals in Marin County and elsewhere in the Bay Area.

Hanf signed the petition as president of Private Money Management Group LLC, the fund’s manager. The case number is 26-30534.

The bankruptcy follows months of regulatory and legal action against Hanf and his companies. The California Department of Financial Protection and Innovation revoked an affiliate’s lending license in April and issued a desist-and-refrain order in May, finding the firm had omitted material information while it raised more than $100 million from hundreds of investors. Some investors say they have been unable to access their money since December. A federal lawsuit filed in April alleges Hanf-controlled entities funneled $75.8 million in investor money to insiders.

The Marin County District Attorney’s Office is also investigating.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Hessel

item.title: Everyday Encounters: A landscape architect looks to balance ecology and human impact
item.description: Landscape architect Kossen Miller, a senior associate at RHAA, is designing two Tiburon park projects: the former sanitation ponds near McKegney Green and Belveron Mini Park.
item.content:encoded:
Landscape architect Kossen Miller was fishing in the bay at Blackie’s Pasture on June 16 when he reeled in something he didn’t expect.

“I was fishing for striped bass or maybe a halibut or something,” he says. “But I caught a shovelnose guitarfish instead.”

Miller took a couple of photos then sent the creature, a member of the ray family, back to its natural habitat.

“It’s kind of one of those things where you catch it and you’re like, ‘Hmm, I’m just really sorry dude,’” he says. “Like, ‘Back to the sea with you. Do whatever you were doing, you know, shoveling about in the mud.’”

Miller, 44, says his relationship with the natural world informs his work as senior associate at Mill Valley-based RHAA Landscape Architects, where he’s working on two Tiburon park projects. The town has contracted with RHAA to come up with final designs for new amenities at the site of the former sanitation ponds near McKegney Green and at Belveron Mini Park. Preliminary concepts include a basketball court, beach volleyball court and outdoor lounge at the former ponds site, while potential upgrades to the mini park include a nature-based play area, an expanded grass lawn and habitats for birds and pollinators.

Miller says the new amenities near McKegney Green will be “awesome.” He says he imagines families out there on sunny days.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
item.media:description: Landscape architect Kossen Miller, a senior associate at RHAA Landscape Architects, hooked a shovelnose guitarfish at Blackie’s Pasture on June 16. Miller is designing new amenities for two Tiburon parks, including the former sanitation ponds site next to McKegney Green. The Blackie the Horse statue stands in the pasture behind him. (Tyler Callister / The Ark)

item.title: Tiburon's Petite Left Bank closes as restaurant group shuts down
item.description: Petite Left Bank in downtown Tiburon has closed, ending the Left Bank restaurant group's presence across the Bay Area.
item.content:encoded:
Petite Left Bank, the scaled-down French brasserie in downtown Tiburon, closed June 22 as its parent company abruptly shut down all seven of its Bay Area restaurants.

A person who answered the phone at Petite Left Bank, granted anonymity because they were not authorized to speak for the restaurant, confirmed the closure June 22 but said it planned to serve customers that evening. An attempt to book a reservation through OpenTable for June 23 returned a message saying the restaurant had closed.

“We are deeply grateful for your support and for the opportunity to serve you,” the message read in part. “Thank you for being part of our story.”

The closure marks the second time in a month that one of downtown Tiburon’s two celebrated-chef restaurants has lost its founding operator. Petite Left Bank carried the name of Roland Passot and the nearby Bungalow Kitchen was built by Michael Mina, both chefs who have won James Beard Awards and Michelin stars. The two restaurants sit in buildings owned by the same landlord, A&C Ventures, which had sought to remake downtown as a fine-dining destination by recruiting chefs with national reputations.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez
item.media:description: Petite Left Bank in downtown Tiburon, seen here June 23, and several other restaurants operated by Vine Hospitality announced their staggered shutdowns June 22, with the Tiburon eatery closing that same day after nearly four years in business. (Francisco Martinez / The Ark)

item.title: Tiburon teen booked on kidnap, robbery after fight with girlfriend
item.description: An 18-year-old Tiburon man was arrested last week on suspicion of kidnapping, robbery, child cruelty and false imprisonment after an argument with his 17-year-old girlfriend.
item.content:encoded:
An 18-year-old Tiburon man was arrested last week on suspicion of kidnapping, robbery, child cruelty and false imprisonment after an argument with his 17-year-old girlfriend, police said.

The man surrendered at the station with his attorney just before 7 p.m. June 16 and was booked into Marin County Jail, according to a Tiburon police media statement. Jail records show he is no longer in custody. Police said the case has been forwarded to the Marin County District Attorney’s Office for review, but fficials there did not immediately respond on the case’s status by The Ark’s press deadline. No case information was available through online court records.

By policy, The Ark generally does not name suspects before they have been formally charged.

Police said the incident began shortly after 2 a.m. June 15, when the couple argued in a parked car near Heathcliff and Lyford Drives. When the girl tried to get out, the suspect allegedly put the car in reverse and accelerated while her door was still open, preventing her from leaving, police said. She tried to call 911, but he took her phone, according to police. The car stopped on Avenida Miraflores near Del Mar Middle School, about 2½ miles away, where she got out.

According to Detective Sgt. Rob Law, the girl ran to a nearby home and knocked, but no one answered. She then walked alone to the Tiburon Police Department, about a mile away, to report what happened.

Officers were first sent to Avenida Miraflores about 2:20 a.m., after the girl’s father called to report her missing, Law said. The man had gone to the father’s home in Mill Valley to say the girl had fled the vehicle, and the two then drove to Avenida Miraflores to look for her.

Officers arrived at 2:24 a.m. and contacted the father and the man, Law said. By then, the girl’s phone had been returned to the father. Law said the man told officers the incident was verbal, and because the girl had not yet been found or interviewed, he was not detained.

At 2:30 a.m., while officers were still on Avenida Miraflores, they learned the girl had reached the police station on foot. Officers returned with the father to interview her and had asked the man to meet them at the station, but he never arrived, Law said. Over the next several hours, officers tried to reach him by phone and at his home before coordinating his surrender.

He was served with an emergency protective order barring him from contacting the gi...
item.dc:creator: Kevin Hessel

item.title: Belvedere budget funds police hiring, lifts staff pay
item.description: The budget includes three years of funding to restore a seventh police position, while the raises follow a study that found Belvedere paying below its Marin neighbors.
item.content:encoded:
Belvedere’s fiscal 2026-2027 budget restores a long-deferred officer position, refills a vacant police sergeant position and raises pay across the city’s workforce after the first salary review in more than 15 years found it paying below its Marin neighbors.

The City Council on June 8 unanimously adopted the $14.49 million budget. The largest in city history, ahead of the July 1 start of the new fiscal year.

The same night, the council approved a two-year contract raising City Manager Robert Zadnik’s salary 12.2% to $267,700 and gave initial approval to three-year contracts for the Police Department and other employees.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister

item.title: Reed Union School District puts $115M bond measure on ballot
item.description: Bond money could go toward replacing portable classrooms, installing air conditioning and upgrading roofs, playgrounds and arts spaces on all three campuses.
item.content:encoded:
The Reed Union School District will ask voters to approve a $115 million bond measure this November to fund facilities upgrades across all three of its campuses.

The district’s board of trustees at its June 8 meeting unanimously approved placing the measure on the Nov. 3 ballot. At the same meeting, the board unanimously approved spending up to $48,050 on a public-information campaign ahead of the election.

Board member Afsaneh Zolfaghari noted that forwarding the bond measure to the ballot was the culmination of four years of discussion and community engagement, including the district’s work to come up with a long-range facilities plan intended to guide building projects at Reed and Bel Aire elementary schools and Del Mar Middle School over the next 10-15 years.

The proposed $115 million bond would charge a tax of $30 per $100,000 of assessed value on properties in the district, which includes Tiburon, Belvedere and east Corte Madera. The district would net about $6.2 million annually. For the median district home assessment of about $2.14 million, the tax would be about $635 annually.

If the bond measure wins the 55% voter approval it needs to pass, the tax would be levied starting in fiscal year 2027-2028 and would run through 2061. The tax amount would likely decrease slightly in the bond’s later years as the district’s debt service decreases.
item.dc:creator: Emily Lavin

item.title: Reed district projects $1.2 mil deficit on $34.5 mil budget
item.description: The Reed Union School District is projecting a $1.2 million deficit in the coming fiscal year as salaries rise and the cost of arts and enrichment programs grows.
item.content:encoded:
The Reed Union School District is projecting a $1.2 million deficit in fiscal 2027 as it provides 3% raises to teachers and staff and plans to spend more than $1 million to close a persistent budget gap for the arts, library and technology programs partially funded by the Foundation for Reed Schools.

The district’s board of trustees voted unanimously at its June 8 meeting to approve the $34.5 million budget for the 2026-2027 fiscal year, which begins July 1.

The budget projects about $33 million in revenue. The district will transfer $510,000 in revenue from tenants at the district-owned Granada campus in east Corte Madera into the general fund and will transfer out $200,000 to its California Employers’ Retiree Benefit Trust, leaving the $1.2 million deficit.

While the district is projecting slight increases in its major sources of revenue, property and parcel taxes, it’s not expecting that to be enough to keep pace with its expenditures next year, forcing it to dip into its general fund to make up the difference.
item.dc:creator: Emily Lavin

item.title: Belvedere grants limited extension for Mallard Pointe redevelopment
item.description: The Belvedere City Council granted the Mallard Pointe developer a shorter extension than it sought, giving it until March 2027 to advance the 40-unit project.
item.content:encoded:
The Belvedere City Council has granted the Mallard Pointe developer more time to complete the steps required to subdivide the property, but far less than it sought. The decision followed a lengthy hearing marked by skeptical questioning from councilmembers and warnings from residents about the risks of demolishing the existing buildings before other pieces of the project are in place.

The council voted unanimously June 8 to extend the tentative map for the 40-unit Mallard Pointe development at 1-22 Mallard Road to March 19, 2027. A tentative map is the city’s preliminary approval to divide a property into building lots, which will let developer Thompson Dorfman Partners sell some of the single-family homes individually — sales it’s counting on to help make the project financially viable. The developer must meet the tentative map’s conditions and record a final map before construction can begin.

The action means the long-debated redevelopment remains alive but under a tighter deadline. The project will replace 22 existing units between City Hall and Belvedere Lagoon with a mix of an apartment building, townhomes, duplexes and single-family homes. Four units must be set aside as affordable for low- and very-low-income households for 55 years.

Thompson Dorfman had asked for a three-year extension, to May 13, 2029. City staff recommended a shorter, two-year extension, to May 13, 2028. The council chose an even shorter time frame, deliberately setting the map to expire the same day as the project’s design review approval.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
item.media:description: A rendering looking north shows homes proposed for the Mallard Pointe development, which would replace 22 units with 40 at 1-22 Mallard Road in Belvedere. The City Council on June 8 extended the project's tentative subdivision map to March 19, 2027. (Sutton Suzuki Architects)

item.title: Summer kickoff on Tiburon's Main Street
item.description: Tiburon's Main Street filled with crowds June 12 for the first Friday Nights on Main festival of the season, timed with the last day of school for Reed Union.
item.content:encoded: Hundreds of people filled lower Main Street in Tiburon on June 12 for the season’s first Friday Nights on Main festival, timed with the last day of school for the Reed Union School District. The Tiburon Peninsula Chamber of Commerce closed the street to traffic for live music from resident Peter Gillespie’s class-rock cover band Tiburon Local Band and children’s activities including face-painting, as well as food and drinks from local restaurants. The chamber will host a second Friday Night on Main on Aug. 21.(Ted McDonnell / For The Ark)
item.dc:creator: The Ark

item.title: Decision due this week on whether Point Tiburon plan can advance
item.description: A Berkeley developer's plan for nine townhomes in the Point Tiburon Plaza parking lot faces a June 18 completeness deadline that could move the project toward approval.
item.content:encoded:
A Berkeley developer’s third bid to build nine townhomes on the Point Tiburon Plaza parking lot faces a town deadline this week that could move the project toward approval.

Tiburon senior planner Kristin Teiche said the town must decide by June 18 whether Black Oak Ventures’ application at 1801 Tiburon Blvd. is complete. The date falls 30 days after the firm’s May 19 resubmission.

A complete finding would advance a 60-day clock toward approval and require the town to prepare a notice of approved application, with any conditions, by July 17, Teiche said. If the application is deemed incomplete, Black Oak would have to resubmit, restarting the process.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez

item.title: Clothes dryer sparks Strawberry house fire; resident and pet forced out
item.description: A clothes dryer started a house fire on Ricardo Road in Strawberry, displacing a resident and their pet, fire officials said.
item.content:encoded:
A clothes dryer ignited a house fire on Ricardo Road in Strawberry over the weekend, displacing a resident and their pet, according to the Southern Marin Fire District.

Fire crews responded about 5 p.m. June 13 and found a garage fully ablaze, with flames spreading into the home and attic, the district said. Firefighters knocked down the fire, searched the home and cut openings to release smoke and heat, bringing it under control in about 15 minutes.

Crews contained the fire by about 5:15 p.m. No injuries were reported.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Callister
item.media:description: Firefighters work to control a house fire on Ricardo Road in Strawberry on June 13. A clothes dryer ignited the blaze, which destroyed the garage and forced a resident and their pet from the home. No one was injured. (via Christian Michael)

item.title: New Business: Nautical clothing brand opens first West Coast outpost on Main Street
item.description: Owner Wayne George said Tiburon's maritime history made it a natural fit for the brand's expansion. He bought the Marblehead store in 2000 from the founding family.
item.content:encoded:
Tiburon is roughly 2,700 miles from Marblehead, Massachusetts, but Wayne George says the two towns have a lot in common, including a tradition of and appreciation for maritime culture.

George is the owner of F.L. Woods, a clothing brand with maritime and nautical inspirations that he says has become a mainstay on Massachusetts’ North Shore.

“We’re a tradition in Marblehead,” George says. “Like, people come from all over to come to this old, creaky store that’s been in the same building since the ’30s, and they know that they can always find something great … for Mother’s Day or for Father’s Day or for themselves.”

The store offers an array of men’s and women’s clothing, including quarter-zip sweaters and mariner jackets as well as shorts, hats and belts, plus mementos like postcards and navigational charts.

The Main Street outpost will be overseen by George’s son and Tiburon resident Josh George and Josh’s partner, Marie Bergsund, the granddaughter of former Tiburon Mayor Joan Bergsund.
item.dc:creator: Francisco Martinez
item.media:description: Tiburon residents Marie Bergsund and Josh George (right) are the managers of F.L. Woods, the maritime-theme clothing store owned by Wayne George (center) that is now open at 21A Main St. in Tiburon. The store is the second after its original in Marblehead, Massachusetts, which opened in 1938. (Francisco Martinez / The Ark_
